title: The Best Botox Injection Depths | Botox Injection Depth Guide
filename: The Best Botox Injection Depths ｜ Botox Injection Depth Guide.en.vtt
excerpt: So this is every injector's nightmare to see one of your patients with a complication like this and what's the primary reason why this sort of complication happens I believe it's to do with depth of injection so this show we're discussing all the right depths to inject and what d…

segments.text: so this is every injector's nightmare to see one of your patients with a complication like this and what's the primary reason why this sort of complication happens I believe it's to do with depth of injection so this show we're discussing all the right depths to inject and what does depth actually mean when I say superficial deep or intermediate so that you can inject with more confidence and Clarity and get hopefully fewer side effects so what exactly causes an eyelid ptosis so always worth remembering that your Anatomy is the foundation of understanding this and injecting safely eyelid ptosis is not caused by injecting the eyelid it's caused by getting botuline toxin into the orbit where the levator palpable muscle that lifts the eyelid actually lives this muscle actually runs all the way to the back of the orbit in order to affect it you need to get botchline toxin behind the orbital membrane which means it's a deep injection so what are the downsides of injecting too deeply now remember this is anatomy specific so there are parts of the face we're injecting deeply is

more safe and there are other parts of the face where it's more risky so we just need to know about the common injection positions and where safe is deep and where safe is not deep now in particular when we're treating around the eye and the lateral corrugator which is just above your mid-papillary line the muscle itself is right near or inserted into the skin so anything beneath that is risk without reward and it so happens that there are also little foramens the passages through which the blood vessels nerves and arteries all pass from the orbit are very close to those deep injection points if you were to go deep this is why injecting deeply above the eye is risky there are other parts of the face where it may be more effective or even safer to inject deeper for example the master muscle can sometimes be a place that's better to inject deeper but similarly also the chin and the medial corrugator and this is because of the anatomy of the muscles the medial corrugator to use that as an example is directly attached to the Bone immediately which means if you're deep you're right in the middle of the muscle

I usually inject three quarters of the way and I wouldn't say it's periosteal injection but it's definitely deeper than lateral and this is because the muscle lives there effectively want to be injecting the muscle and not underneath the muscle so where else might it be particularly dangerous to inject too deeply probably the most common area I see new injectors make a mistake would be around the orbicular circuli particularly the inferior lateral part of the eye so if you're trying to reduce wrinkles here but you go all the way through the muscle which remember is the most superficial thing after a very small layer of fat so you have a tiny bit of skin tiny layer of fat and then the muscle so you only really need to pop the bevel of the needle in one to two millimeters and you're right on top of the muscle if you keep going you actually go through the fat and the next muscle that you come to is the origin of the zygomaticus major so this in theory can lead to a droopy smile particularly if you're too inferior so injecting deeply here is particularly risky for an asymmetrical smile which is a terrible side effect so where do we need to make sure that we

are definitely superficial probably one of the best examples of this would be the superior aspect of the orbicularis oculi muscle just underneath the eyebrow when you're trying to create a little bit of an eyebrow lift in this area you're injecting just through the dermis right on top of the muscle underneath that muscle a thin layer of fat and then you're getting into the area of the orbital membrane and the orbit itself which is why you may get complications there is no effect benefit from injecting deeper there are only risks whereas when you're superficial you actually get the muscle you're trying to get so this is the most one of the most important areas to be very superficial the same concept really applies anywhere when you're trying to inject the orbicularis oculi muscle now I know many people want to have specific millimeter by millimeter depths when I say superficial or deep it may help to realize that quite often what I'm thinking about is relative to the main layers of the face we have the dermis the hypodermic fat usually muscle and then the periosteum just to simplify things so very deep injections are near the periosteum and very superficial

injections are near the dermis intermediate ones are somewhere in between now if you're looking at different aspects of the face the difference in millimeters is actually quite huge so I'm not actually giving measurements because it's all relative to the place that you're injecting and you need to know your layers and the anatomy and what it feels like to be in the different layers take for example the forehead if I say a superficial injection in the forehead I mean the dermis how do you know that you're in the dermis well as you inject you'll see the skin blanch it's also quite stiff at the point of injecting because you've got so much more connective tissue resisting the movement of the plunger as you try and inject but if you go a little bit deeper you go into hypodermic fat and here there's a sudden ease as the needle has passed through the connective tissue and you end up with an easier to inject position which is probably just on top of the muscle you can go deeper still and I'm only talking two or three millimeters and you should touch the periosteum which point you are underneath the muscle and you can feel that because the bone feels

firm those will be the three layers of the forehead described as superficial intermediate and deep which is very different if you're injecting the master and if I'm if I was describing the master muscle and you inject it superficially you would be either in the dermis or The Superficial part of the muscle intermediate be halfway through but it might be a full needle depth of a BD syringe and a deep injection you'd be compressing the muscle until you feel periosteum but they're still really relative to whether you're on the bone in the muscle or fat or in The hypodermis Superficial deep or intermediate so we've just discussed the master let's have a look at a case and try and figure out whether this was a superficial or a deep injection now in this video what you can see is Contracting muscle superficially so there's a popping out superficially of the muscle which tells me that what actually happened was that the injection was relatively deep in a relatively big Master muscle so we've relaxed the muscle in it in the deeper plane but more superficially there's an area of muscle that's still Contracting which

you see as a bulge but in the case of the master muscle here there are two different theories that people talk about and I am a big fan actually that it's more likely the Deep injection rather than superficial injections that are causing this because what I can see is superficial activity and deep relaxation the other theory that I've seen put forward is that as you inject more superficially you get bulging of the deeper muscles through the surface like a herniating out but to me this seems much less likely given that you get it within the first couple of weeks that the muscle is atrophying and allowing muscle to protrude for me it's quite clear that you have superficial contractility which means the simple cure is to inject a little bit more superficially so that you get those fibers but be warned a lot of these patients present in the first week and all you need to do is just wait because it settles down on its own as more activity of the botronym toxin actually takes place because you it seems to be around three weeks that it doesn't look anywhere near the same way in many of my

patients who've come back with this with an early follow-up expect this to be a potential side effect in the first three days or so give it more time and in most cases it settles and it happens more often when the muscle is Big so when you get those really big Master muscles you're much more likely to have this as a side effect to when they're more moderate now let's have a look at the correct depths on a real patient I'm going to go through each area and tell you how we know what depth are at and what my reasons are for being at that particular depth proceris deep medial corrugator deep middle of the corrugator intermediate lateral corrugator superficial frontalis deep or intermediate orbicular succuli all the way around superficial so glabella medial aspect is deep because that's the depth of the muscle only about three quarters of the way in

it's a relatively long needle and we do four units while compressing the orbit the next injection is intermediate depth three units and this one the most superficial just into the insertion point of the muscle you can see a little bit of blanching there that's correct muscle relatively deep originates from the bone four units metal corrugator deeper injection all units frontalis deep injection two units [Music]
[Music] foreign [Music] depths with botuline toxin in different areas of the face if you want to learn more about dermal filler depths make sure you watch this video here [Music]
